config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. In my view, society matters a lot, but it should not automatically override individual rights, because once the state or the majority can easily dismiss personal freedoms, abuse becomes very hard to stop. A healthy society is built by protecting people's dignity, speech, and basic liberties, even when those rights are inconvenient. From my perspective, especially in places where power can be misused, individual rights are the safeguard that keeps "the common good" from becoming a tool of oppression.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in China, social stability and collective interests are very important, and people do benefit when public order is protected, but I have also seen how easily "the common good" can be used to silence legitimate personal needs and criticism. If individual rights are treated as secondary in principle, then the line between necessary coordination and abuse becomes too easy to cross. A good society should balance both, but it should never assume that the individual can be sacrificed whenever the majority or authorities claim a higher purpose.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I understand the value of gotong royong and social harmony, but I also know that appeals to "the common good" can be misused to pressure people into silence or obedience. The point made about balance is important, but balance only works if individual rights are treated as real limits, not as privileges that society can cancel whenever it wants. A society is healthier when it protects people first, because then its collective goals are more legitimate and less likely to become abuse.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective in China, I understand why people emphasize stability, order, and collective goals, because these can prevent chaos and help many people live better lives, but I do not think that gives society the right to override basic personal rights whenever it finds them inconvenient. The danger is that once individual rights are treated as secondary, criticism, privacy, and fair treatment can all be dismissed in the name of harmony or progress. To me, a strong society is one that can pursue the common good without asking individuals to give up their dignity and protection.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over individual rights as a general principle. From my perspective in Indonesia, I value gotong royong and social harmony, but I have also seen how quickly “the public interest” can be used to pressure people to stay quiet or accept unfair treatment. The point about stability is real, but stability without firm limits on power can become a polite word for control. A society is strongest when it protects individual dignity first, because then its collective goals have real legitimacy instead of relying on coercion.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why people value stability, coordination, and the common good, because disorder can hurt ordinary people very quickly, but I also know that once individual rights are treated as secondary, it becomes too easy for power to excuse almost anything. The argument about social harmony sounds reasonable, yet in practice it can hide unfairness, silence criticism, and pressure people to accept treatment they would never choose freely. A good society should protect the common interest, but it should do so with clear limits that keep basic rights from being sacrificed whenever authorities or the majority claim a higher purpose.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
